sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.

You are still on the same level.

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1 has a gray background with 3 charcoal-framed blue squares arranged in a triangle, a blue horizontal bar with gray fill in the center, and a white square above a green rectangle near the bottom center (likely the player avatar). Pink bar at bottom row 63 is a HUD element. The player appears to be at the white/green region around row 48-51, col 32-35.
- Goal model: Unclear yet. Likely need to navigate the player to interact with the blue-framed squares or the central bar.
- Plan: Probe with movement actions to understand what the player controls and what the goal is.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The board changed after pressing UP. Let me check what changed by comparing the before and after frames. I need to see if the player (white/green) moved or if something else changed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Compare before and after UP action
before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
after = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation

def get_node_map(seg):
    return {n['id']: n for n in seg['nodes']}

before_nodes = get_node_map(before)
after_nodes = get_node_map(after)

print("Before UP - key nodes:")
for nid in [9, 10]:  # white and green
    if nid in before_nodes:
        n = before_nodes[nid]
        print(f"  id={nid}, color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

print("\nAfter UP - key nodes:")
for nid in [9, 10]:
    if nid in after_nodes:
        n = after_nodes[nid]
        print(f"  id={nid}, color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check all nodes for changes
print("\nAll after nodes:")
for n in after['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [9, 10, 11]:
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The white square moved from row 48 to row 44, and the green rectangle moved from row 49-51 to row 45-47. Both moved up by 4 rows. So the player avatar (white square + green rectangle below it) moves when I press direction keys. The white square is the "head" and the green rectangle is the "body".
Now I need to figure out the goal. The player is at col 32-35, and there are blue-framed squares at:
- Left: col 16-19, row 28-31
- Right: col 44-47, row 24-27
- Below center: col 32-35, row 36-39
- Center bar: col 28-39, row 28-31
The player is currently at row 44-47, col 32-35. Let me try to navigate to one of the blue squares to see what happens. Let me try moving up more to reach the center bar or the blue square below it.
Actually, let me think about the layout more carefully:
- Blue bar (node 4): row 28-31, col 28-39
- Blue square below bar (node 7): row 36-39, col 32-35
- Player (white+green): now at row 44-47, col 32-35
The player is directly below the blue square at node 7. Let me move up more to reach it.

Three charcoal-framed blue squares exist at left (row 28-31, col 16-19), right (row 24-27, col 44-47), and below-center (row 36-39, col 32-35). A blue horizontal bar with gray fill is at row 28-31, col 28-39.
Goal model: Likely need to navigate to interact with the blue-framed squares. The player is aligned vertically with the center blue square.
Plan: Move UP to approach the blue square below the bar, then explore further.

# Move UP more to reach the blue square at row 36-39
# Player is at row 44-47, need to get to around row 36
# Each UP moves 4 rows, so 2 more UPs should get us there

</tool_call>
